title: The Three Big Failure Modes
concept: GAN training's general trickiness — two competing losses, balance that can tip either way, bouncing loss curves, eyeballing samples — funnels into three specific, well-documented failure modes: mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and training instability.

exercises.prompt: Recap: why do GAN practitioners periodically look directly at generated sample images instead of trusting loss numbers alone?

options.text: Because loss numbers are noisy and don't reliably reflect what the generated images actually look like
Because computing loss numbers for GANs is technically impossible
Because looking at images trains the discriminator faster

exercises.explain: Right. You now have the full 'this is genuinely trickier' picture. Today: naming the three specific problems behind it.

cards.title: Naming the trail hazards ahead
cards.body: A good trail guide doesn't just say "the hike is hard" — it names the specific hazards you'll actually encounter: loose rock here, a stream crossing there, a steep drop past the ridge. You've now got the general "this is genuinely trickier" picture for GAN training. Time to name the three specific hazards.

cards.title: Three named problems, three coming lessons
cards.body: Everything covered so far — competing losses, balance breaking in either direction, bouncing loss curves, eyeballing samples — funnels into three specific, well-documented failure modes that GAN practitioners run into constantly: mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and training instability.

cards.title: A field guide, not just a warning sign
cards.body: Instead of one vague sign reading "danger ahead," a field guide names each hazard specifically so you know what you're looking at when you see it. Mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and instability are the three entries in the GAN field guide — each one gets its own close look in the units right after this.

cards.title: A quick preview of each
cards.body: Mode collapse: the generator finds a few outputs that reliably fool the discriminator and stops exploring, producing the same handful of results over and over. Vanishing gradients: the discriminator gets so confident that the generator's learning signal shrinks toward nothing. Training instability: the two networks oscillate back and forth without ever settling into a steady rhythm.

cards.title: "Are these three rare edge cases?"
cards.body: It's easy to assume these are obscure problems that only show up in unusual setups. In reality, these three failure modes are so common that most real GAN training runs brush up against at least one of them — which is exactly why each one gets its own dedicated unit next.

cards.title: Compare
cards.body: From general symptom to specific, named causes:

left.text:
Two competing losses
Hard to read progress
Something's often off

exercises.prompt: Which three failure modes does this lesson name as the specific problems coming up next?

options.text: Mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and training instability
Vanishing gradients, exploding gradients, and dead neurons
Mode collapse, overfitting, and class imbalance

exercises.explain: Right — these three are the named hazards the next few units each tackle in depth.

exercises.prompt: True or false: mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and training instability are rare edge cases that only show up in unusual GAN setups.
exercises.explain: False — they're common enough that most real GAN training runs brush up against at least one of them.

exercises.sentence: In mode collapse, the generator finds a few outputs that reliably fool the discriminator and ___ exploring, producing the same handful of results over and over.
exercises.explain: It settles into a narrow set of safe outputs instead of continuing to explore the full range of possibilities.

exercises.prompt: Which description matches 'vanishing gradients' as previewed in this lesson?

options.text: The discriminator gets so confident that the generator's learning signal shrinks toward nothing
The generator produces a wider and wider variety of convincing outputs over time
Both networks stop training at exactly the same moment on purpose

exercises.explain: That's the vanishing-gradients pattern: an overconfident discriminator leaves the generator with almost no useful signal.

exercises.prompt: You now know the three big failure modes waiting ahead: mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and training instability. Which one comes first?

options.text: Mode collapse — where the generator finds a few outputs that always work and stops exploring
None of them — GAN training is actually stable once you understand it

exercises.explain: Exactly — up next, Unit 206: Mode Collapse, the first of the three failure modes, where the generator finds a shortcut and stops exploring.
